mappings.rationale: Directly covers the Aztec state, its military campaigns, administrative techniques, and culture and society up to the Spanish conquest.
Directly covers Maya civilization in both Classic and Post-Classic periods, including urban settlements, temple-pyramids, the calendar, writing system, and the civilization's collapse.
The Spanish conquest of the Aztec Empire and the broader context of European conquest of Meso-America are central to the section's coverage of the Post-Classic Period.
The section relies extensively on archaeological evidence for understanding Olmec, Maya, Toltec, and Aztec civilizations, including ceremonial architecture and pottery.
The Aztec state's extension of rule over the Valley of Mexico and the Toltec and Chimu empires exemplify pre-Columbian imperial systems.
Late Pleistocene and Early Hunter peoples, the development of agriculture from c. 6500 BC, and pre-ceramic cultures connect to broader prehistoric questions.
The ethnography of Meso-America, including Aztec political organization, religion, and social structures, involves anthropological frameworks.
Documentary historical sources about Meso-American civilizations and the Spanish conquest form part of the colonial Latin American literary record.
The legend of Quetzalcoatl, Maya religion, and Aztec religious practices represent significant mythological traditions covered in this section.
The indigenous peoples of Meso-America, including their pre-Columbian civilizations and cultural achievements, are part of the broader history of indigenous peoples of the Americas.
